title: Measuring What Matters: KPIs for AI-Assisted Sales

questions.question: What are the 5 metrics in the solo founder's acquisition dashboard?

options.text: Open rate, click rate, bounce rate, unsubscribe rate, spam rate
Prospects added/week, emails sent/week, reply rate %, conversations booked/week, pipeline value/month
Revenue, profit, churn, NPS, customer lifetime value
LinkedIn views, website visits, demo requests, trials started, conversions

questions.explanation: The 5-metric dashboard tracks: Prospects Added/Week (pipeline fuel), Emails Sent/Week (activity baseline), Reply Rate % (efficiency indicator), Conversations Booked/Week (conversion metric), and Pipeline Value/Month (outcome metric). Everything else is either a vanity metric or a diagnostic tool.

questions.question: Why should solo founders ignore email open rates as a primary metric?

options.text: Open rates are too high to be useful
Apple Mail Privacy Protection and other clients pre-load images, inflating open rates by 30-50%
Open rates only matter for marketing emails, not sales emails
Open rates are only available on paid plans

questions.explanation: Open rates are increasingly unreliable due to Apple Mail Privacy Protection and other email clients that pre-load tracking pixels, inflating rates by 30-50%. The lesson advises ignoring open rates entirely and focusing on reply rates as the true engagement metric.

questions.question: Using the pipeline math formula, if your revenue goal is $10K/month with $2K deal size and 25% close rate, how many conversations do you need per month?
questions.explanation: The math: $10K revenue goal / $2K deal size = 5 deals needed. 5 deals / 25% close rate = 20 conversations needed per month (or 5 per week). This 'working backwards from revenue' approach ensures your activity targets are grounded in actual business goals.

questions.question: You should check your acquisition dashboard three times daily to stay on top of performance.
questions.explanation: False. Checking your dashboard 3x/day creates anxiety and doesn't give enough data for decisions. The lesson recommends checking once per week, same day/time, and letting data accumulate. The 60-second rule: your dashboard should answer three questions instantly — Am I feeding the machine? Is it working? Is it producing revenue?

questions.question: Calculate your personal pipeline math by working backwards from your monthly revenue goal. What is your deal size, close rate, reply-to-conversation rate, and reply rate? How many emails do you need to send per month to hit your target?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response includes specific numbers for each step of the pipeline math (revenue goal, deal size, close rate, conversations needed, reply-to-conversation rate, replies needed, reply rate, emails needed). Look for realistic assumptions and correct mathematical logic connecting each step.
